items.text: Chapter Eight
Host Plants of Tuta absoluta
Principal Host, Confirmed Hosts, Alternative Hosts, Experimental Hosts, Suspected Hosts, and Weeds
Introduction

Tuta absoluta is closely associated with plants belonging to the family Solanaceae, particularly tomato. Nevertheless, the female can lay eggs, or the larvae can feed and develop, on a number of other cultivated and wild plants.

Not all plants on which the insect has been recorded have the same degree of importance. An adult moth may be found on a plant used only as a resting site; a female may lay an egg on a plant that cannot support larval development; or a larva may begin feeding but die before reaching the pupal stage.
Therefore, a plant should not be considered a true complete host merely because of finding:

items: A single egg on its leaves.
A young larva that was not followed through pupation.
Field damage for which the causative insect was not confirmed.

items.text: A true host is confirmed when the insect can complete its life cycle on the plant from egg to adult, with the resulting individuals capable of survival and reproduction.
Tuta absoluta is classified as a relatively oligophagous insect:
Oligophagous insect
Most of its true host range is concentrated within the family Solanaceae, especially the genus:
Solanum
Tomato remains the most preferred and suitable plant for reproduction and population increase.
Criteria for Classifying a Plant as a Host
Classification of a plant as a host of Tuta absoluta depends on a sequence of evidence.
Female Acceptance of the Plant
This is measured through:

items: Preference when more than one choice is available.

items.text: Oviposition alone is insufficient to prove that the plant is a complete host.
Egg Hatching
A female may accept a plant for oviposition, but the eggs may fail to hatch because of:
Larval Survival
Larvae must be able to:
Pupation
If a larva begins feeding but dies before pupation, the plant is not considered a complete host.
Adult Emergence
Emergence of a healthy adult moth provides strong evidence that the plant can support the life cycle.
Reproductive Capacity of the Resulting Insect
A high-quality host produces:

items: Offspring capable of initiating a new generation.

items.text: Repeated Field Infestation
Evidence becomes stronger when infestation recurs:
Categories of Host Plants
Plants associated with Tuta absoluta in this chapter are divided into the following categories:

A “confirmed host” describes the strength of scientific evidence, whereas an “alternative host” describes the ecological role played by the plant in sustaining the pest when tomato is absent. A plant may therefore be scientifically confirmed as a host and simultaneously function ecologically as an alternative host.
First: The Principal Host
Tomato
Solanum lycopersicum
Tomato is the principal and most preferred host of Tuta absoluta.
The insect generally achieves on tomato:

items: The highest number of eggs laid.
The fastest rate of population increase.

items.text: Comparative studies have shown that tomato clearly exceeds potato, eggplant, and black nightshade in oviposition preference and biological performance of the insect. [1, 2]
Reasons for Tomato Preference
Tomato preference is associated with factors including:

items: Volatile compounds released from the leaves.
Suitability of mesophyll tissue for larval feeding.
Ease of penetrating the leaf epidermis.
Evolutionary adaptation of the insect to plants of the genus Solanum.

items.text: The female uses its antennae and sensory receptors on the legs and palpi to recognize the plant before laying eggs.
Infested Plant Parts
Larvae can attack most aerial parts, including:
Roots are not a feeding host for the larvae, although pupae may occur in soil surrounding the plant.
Volunteer Tomato Plants
Volunteer tomato plants arise from:

items: Seeds remaining from the previous season.

items.text: Under field conditions, these plants behave as highly dangerous weeds because they provide the principal host outside the normal production season.
They may carry:
Volunteer tomatoes are among the most important components of the green bridge between cropping seasons.
Second: Confirmed Hosts
A confirmed host is a plant on which Tuta absoluta has been shown to complete its life cycle, either in the field or under controlled experiments.
Potato
Solanum tuberosum
Potato is a confirmed host, but it is less preferred and less suitable than tomato.
Larvae mainly infest:
Tuta absoluta is not known as a principal pest of potato tubers in the same manner as the potato tuber moth:
Phthorimaea operculella
The two species must therefore not be confused.
In one comparative experiment, mean developmental duration was:
Survival was:
These results show that potato can support the complete life cycle, but is a lower-quality host than tomato.
Eggplant
Solanum melongena
Eggplant is a confirmed host on which the insect can complete development.
However, performance is generally lower than on tomato and is characterized by:
In one study, mean developmental duration on eggplant was approximately:
25.9 days
And survival was approximately:
Compared with 84% on tomato. [3]
Low survival does not mean that eggplant is not a host; it means that it is a less suitable host.
African Eggplant
Solanum aethiopicum
Tuta absoluta has been shown to complete its life cycle on African eggplant.
In a comparative experiment:
Performance was lower than on tomato but better than on some cultivars of common eggplant in the same experiment. [3]
Pepino or Melon Pear
Solanum muricatum
Pepino is a confirmed experimental host on which the insect can:
Its field importance is greater in regions where it is cultivated near tomato or inside protected production systems. [4]
Black Nightshade
Solanum nigrum
Black nightshade is one of the most important confirmed wild hosts.
On this plant, Tuta absoluta can:
Several experiments have demonstrated that the plant can support the complete life cycle, although oviposition preference and overall performance are generally higher on tomato. [1, 5]
Its importance is increased because it grows:

items: Around drainage channels and irrigation canals.

items.text: Hairy Nightshade
Solanum sarrachoides
The insect has been shown to complete its life cycle on this plant in experiments conducted on wild solanaceous species.
It is considered an experimentally confirmed host and may contribute to pest persistence in regions where it occurs naturally. [5]
Wild Eggplant
Solanum coagulans
This species has been recorded as a confirmed host in host-range studies, and the insect completed development on it.
Its field importance depends on:

items: Presence of several living pest stages on the plant. [1]

items.text: Tree Tobacco
Nicotiana glauca
Tree tobacco is a wild plant in the family Solanaceae.
Recent review of host records indicates that Tuta absoluta can complete its life cycle on it. [4]
It becomes important when growing:

items: During periods when the principal crop is absent.

items.text: Indian Nightshade
Solanum indicum or closely related species
A confirmed infestation of Tuta absoluta on Indian nightshade was reported in a study published in 2025.

This record represents a recent addition to the list of solanaceous plants associated with the pest, but its economic and geographical importance requires broader investigation beyond the initial confirmation of infestation. [6]
Third: Alternative Hosts
An alternative host is a plant used by the insect when tomato is absent or less abundant and that helps the pest survive and move between cropping seasons.
An alternative host is usually biologically confirmed but performs an ecological function in sustaining the pest population.
Principal Alternative Hosts
Volunteer Tomato Plants
Solanum lycopersicum
These are the most dangerous alternative hosts because they combine:
Black Nightshade
Solanum nigrum
This is the most important alternative weed because it is a confirmed host and persists inside and around fields.
Potato
Solanum tuberosum
It acts as an alternative host when potato and tomato planting seasons overlap.
Eggplant
Solanum melongena
It functions as an alternative host in long-duration crops, particularly when tomato is absent.
African Eggplant
Solanum aethiopicum
It may maintain part of the pest population in regions where it is cultivated.
Pepino
Solanum muricatum
It may act as an alternative host within greenhouses or mixed-production areas.
Tree Tobacco
Nicotiana glauca
It acts as a wild alternative host, particularly when it remains green during periods without tomato.
Wild Solanum Species
Some wild species may function as local pest reservoirs depending on their suitability and distribution.
Fourth: Experimental or Poorly Suitable Hosts

An experimental host is a plant tested in the laboratory or in cages on which oviposition, feeding, or partial development occurred, but whose ability to support a complete life cycle or a persistent field population is limited or inconsistent.
Pepper
Capsicum annuum and other Capsicum species
Oviposition or infestation has been recorded on pepper in some regions, but experimental results have not been consistent.
In a study involving 27 crops, the insect was unable to complete its life cycle on the tested Capsicum species. [3]
In other experiments:

items: A small number of eggs were laid.
Pepper was less preferred than tomato.
A large proportion of individuals failed to complete development.

items.text: Pepper is therefore classified as a weak or experimental host rather than a principal host.
Cultivated Tobacco
Nicotiana tabacum
Females can lay some eggs on cultivated tobacco, but preference is low when tomato is available.
In one host-choice study, tobacco was excluded from advanced comparison stages because few eggs were laid on it. [2]
Its ability to support development varies according to:
Aztec Tobacco
Nicotiana rustica
This species has been studied as a potential solanaceous host, but available evidence remains experimental and does not demonstrate broad field importance. [7]
Jimsonweed
Datura stramonium
Jimsonweed appeared in older host lists as an alternative host.
However, recent experiments showed that the insect could not complete its life cycle on it, and some studies recorded either no oviposition or high larval mortality. [4, 8]
It is therefore classified as an incomplete experimental host rather than a confirmed host.
Long-Spined Thorn Apple
Datura ferox
A recent review reported no oviposition on this plant in the evaluated experiments.
There is therefore insufficient evidence to regard it as a true complete host. [4]
Bittersweet Nightshade
Solanum dulcamara

This plant has been tested as an alternative solanaceous host, and varying degrees of development occurred on it, but its field importance remains lower than that of tomato, black nightshade, and potato. [7]
Goji Berry
Lycium barbarum
This plant has been tested in some studies as a potential solanaceous host.
Evidence that it can support stable field generations remains limited, and it is therefore classified among experimental plants. [7]
American Black Nightshade
Solanum americanum
This species has been included among spontaneous plants tested for development of Tuta absoluta.
Its field importance remains limited and variable according to region, and it should not automatically be treated as a confirmed host equivalent to Solanum nigrum. [4]
Tropical Soda Apple
Solanum viarum
Oviposition or partial development occurred on this plant in some studies, but results differ among pest populations.
In one host-choice study, it did not produce the expected oviposition response despite appearing in other reports. [2]
Common Bean
Phaseolus vulgaris
Bean has appeared in some host lists, and oviposition or development has been recorded in local studies.
Other experiments showed that the insect could not complete a stable life cycle on it as it does on plants of the genus Solanum.
It is therefore classified as a local or poorly suitable host rather than a general principal host. [1, 3]
Onion
Allium cepa
In a West African experiment, some larvae began developing on onion leaves but did not reach the pupal stage.
Onion therefore represents an example of a plant permitting partial feeding but not supporting the complete life cycle. [3]
Common Morning Glory
Ipomoea purpurea
This species has recently been tested among spontaneous plants, although it does not belong to the family Solanaceae.
Its importance remains experimental until the following are demonstrated:
Fifth: Suspected Hosts
A suspected host is a plant whose name has appeared in a report or host list but for which sufficient information on completion of the insect’s life cycle is unavailable.
Suspected Plants
These include:
Watermelon
Citrullus lanatus
It has appeared in some lists without sufficient life-cycle data.
Squash and Pumpkin
Cucurbita pepo
Oviposition was recorded in some experiments, but the insect did not complete its life cycle. [4]
Beet
Beta vulgaris
No oviposition was recorded in some tests, and it is therefore not a confirmed host. [4]
Alfalfa
Medicago sativa
It appeared in older lists, but sufficient life-cycle data are unavailable.
Spinach
Spinacia oleracea
No information confirms completion of the life cycle on this plant.
Jatropha
Jatropha curcas
Its name has appeared in host lists but without sufficient data on hatching, development, and pupation.
Spiny Amaranth
Amaranthus spinosus
There is insufficient information demonstrating support of a complete life cycle.
Cocklebur
Xanthium strumarium
It has been reported in field records, but sufficient information on completion of the life cycle is unavailable.
Red Goosefoot
Oxybasis rubra
It has been recorded as an associated wild plant, but complete developmental information is unavailable.
Some Lycium Species
Lycium spp.
Records exist for some species, but evidence of complete life-cycle development differs among species.
Sixth: Plants with Incidental Association
Tuta absoluta may occur on a plant without that plant being a true host.
Forms of Incidental Association
Adult Resting
An adult moth may rest on:
The presence of an adult does not mean that larvae can feed on the plant.
Laying of a Single Egg
A female may lay an egg on an unsuitable plant because of:
Larval Movement
A larva may leave its mine and move to a neighboring plant, then begin limited feeding without completing development.
Fallen Infested Leaf
An infested tomato leaf may fall into another weed, making the larva appear to occur on that weed.
Confusion with Other Leaf Miners
Mines may be caused by:
A host should therefore not be confirmed from mine appearance alone.
Seventh: Weeds and Wild Hosts
Importance of Weeds
Some weeds are important in sustaining the pest between tomato crops.
Host weeds perform the following functions:

items: Forming a green bridge between seasons.
Transferring infestation to newly planted fields.

items.text: However, not all weeds are Tuta hosts, and weeds should not be removed indiscriminately without identification because some non-host plants provide food and shelter for natural enemies.
Confirmed Weeds
Black Nightshade
Solanum nigrum
The most important confirmed weed host of Tuta absoluta.
The insect can complete its entire life cycle on it.
Hairy Nightshade
Solanum sarrachoides
An experimentally confirmed wild host.
Wild Eggplant
Solanum coagulans
Recorded as a confirmed host in host-range studies.
Tree Tobacco
Nicotiana glauca
A confirmed wild host in recent reviews and capable of supporting the complete life cycle.
Volunteer Tomato Plants
Solanum lycopersicum
These are not a separate wild species but are treated under field conditions as highly dangerous weeds.
Experimental Weeds
These include plants on which oviposition or partial development occurred, or that were tested without confirmation of broad field importance.
Jimsonweed
Datura stramonium
The insect does not complete its life cycle on this plant according to recent experiments.
Long-Spined Thorn Apple
Datura ferox
No oviposition was recorded in some tests.
American Black Nightshade
Solanum americanum
Experimental results are variable.
Tropical Soda Apple
Solanum viarum
Its acceptance for oviposition and biological performance are inconsistent among studies.
Bittersweet Nightshade
Solanum dulcamara
It has been tested as a potential host but is less important than confirmed hosts.
Aztec Tobacco
Nicotiana rustica
Its association is experimental and does not demonstrate general field importance.
Goji Berry
Lycium barbarum
Further field confirmation is required.
Common Morning Glory
Ipomoea purpurea
Evidence of association is recent and experimental.
Weeds Recorded Without Complete Confirmation
Field Bindweed
Convolvulus arvensis
It has been recorded in Egypt and Turkey as a plant bearing mines or larvae attributed to Tuta absoluta.
However, evidence is conflicting because other experiments indicated either absence of oviposition or unsuitability of plants in the family Convolvulaceae for pest development. [9, 10]
Field bindweed is therefore classified as:
A locally recorded host requiring confirmation of complete life-cycle development.
It should not be treated as a confirmed host equivalent to black nightshade.
Common Lambsquarters
Chenopodium album
It has been recorded during some field surveys, but suitability experiments showed that it is unsuitable for completion of pest development.
Field records may result from:
Silverleaf Nightshade
Solanum elaeagnifolium

This species belongs to the family Solanaceae and has appeared in weed lists associated with the pest, but evidence that it supports the complete life cycle remains weaker than that available for black nightshade.
Chilean Boxthorn
Lycium chilense
It has been recorded as a wild plant, but complete life-cycle information is unavailable. [4]
Red Goosefoot
Oxybasis rubra
It has appeared in weed lists without sufficient information on completion of the life cycle. [4]
Ranking of Weeds According to Risk
Priority Weeds in Egypt
Priority field inspection in Egypt should focus on:

items: 5. Tree tobacco, Nicotiana glauca, where present.

items.text: The highest priority is assigned to volunteer tomato and black nightshade because evidence that they support the complete life cycle is stronger than for most other weeds.
The Green Bridge
The green bridge refers to the continued presence of living plants that the pest can use between two cropping seasons.
The persistence chain develops as follows:

items: 2. Larvae and pupae remain in crop residues.
4. They move to volunteer tomato, black nightshade, or alternative hosts.
6. A new tomato crop is planted.
7. Moths move into the newly planted crop.

items.text: The risk of a green bridge increases when:

items: Old fields are left after the final harvest.
Potato and eggplant are grown beside tomato.

items.text: Effect of the Host on Insect Performance
Plant species affects:
In one comparative study involving several crops:
The results show that the ability to complete the life cycle does not mean that all plants are equal in quality.
Female Host Selection
The female passes through several stages before oviposition:
Choice studies showed the following general preference order:
Tomato > potato > eggplant > black nightshade
Biological performance of the immature stages was largely consistent with female preference. [2]
However, preference order may vary among regions because of:
Host Plants and Crop Rotation
Crop rotation does not successfully reduce Tuta absoluta if tomato is replaced directly by a suitable solanaceous crop such as:
Rotation becomes more effective when it includes:

items: Absence of plants belonging to the genus Solanum.

items.text: West African experiments showed that the insect could not complete its life cycle on most crops outside the genus Solanum, supporting the importance of a period during which this genus is absent from the production area. [3]
Examination of a Potential Host
When a new plant is suspected, the following steps should be followed:

items: 1. Determine the scientific name of the plant.
2. Photograph the plant and its location.
6. Confirm that the larva is lepidopteran and possesses a head and legs.
11. Confirm the identity of the moth.
12. Repeat the observation on several plants.
13. Evaluate the reproductive capacity of the resulting adult.

items.text: Emergence of an adult moth from a larva that fed on the plant provides stronger evidence than the presence of eggs or mines alone.
Management of Hosts and Weeds
Removal of Volunteer Tomato Plants
They should be removed before they permit development of a new generation.
Management of Black Nightshade
Plants should be inspected inside fields, along margins, and around drainage channels, and infested plants should be removed in a manner that prevents emergence of larvae and adults.
Removal of Crop Residues
Residues should be shredded, buried, or disposed of away from the new crop.
Avoid Leaving Piles of Infested Weeds
Larvae may continue developing within removed plants, then pupate and produce adults.
Nursery Sanitation
Wild solanaceous hosts should be removed from the area surrounding the nursery.
Avoid Indiscriminate Removal
Not every weed should be removed merely because it is present. Some non-host plants may support:
Beneficial non-host plants should be retained when they do not represent a pest source.
Timing of Removal
Adults emerging from an old host must be prevented from moving into the new crop.
Removal should be integrated with:
Common Errors
Error One: Every Plant Bearing a Mine Is a Host
The mine may have been caused by another insect.
Error Two: Oviposition Proves Host Status
A female may lay eggs on a plant that does not permit completion of the life cycle.
Error Three: All Solanaceous Plants Are Equal
Tomato, potato, eggplant, pepper, and jimsonweed differ greatly in suitability.
Error Four: Every Plant Listed in an Old Host List Is Confirmed
Older lists included plants on which only an egg or mine had been recorded.
Error Five: Field Bindweed Is Definitively Confirmed as a Host
Local records exist, but study results are conflicting, and it should not be treated as a complete host without confirmation.
Error Six: Removing Tomato Alone Breaks the Cycle
The pest may persist on volunteer tomato, black nightshade, potato, and eggplant.
Error Seven: Potato Is Unimportant
Potato is a confirmed host, although tomato is more preferred.
Error Eight: All Weeds Must Be Removed
Removal should focus on confirmed and infested hosts while preserving non-host plants that support natural enemies.
Comprehensive Reference Table

rows: Complete cycle, lower performance than tomato
Usually does not complete the cycle

items.text: Chapter Summary
Tomato is the principal and most suitable host of Tuta absoluta, on which the insect achieves the highest oviposition preference, fastest development, highest survival, and greatest reproduction.
Confirmed hosts include:
Volunteer tomato, black nightshade, potato, eggplant, and tree tobacco function as alternative hosts that help the pest persist between cropping seasons.
Pepper, cultivated tobacco, jimsonweed, bean, and several wild plants vary in suitability and should not be treated as confirmed hosts in every region.
Suspected hosts include several non-solanaceous plants that appeared in older lists without proof of complete life-cycle development.
Solanaceous weeds, especially black nightshade and volunteer tomato, are the most important sources of the green bridge between seasons.
Confirmation of a true host depends on completion of the insect’s life cycle, not merely on finding an egg, mine, or larva.
Host management depends on:

items: Managing black nightshade and confirmed wild hosts.
Inspecting weeds around nurseries and fields.
Maintaining a host-free period without plants of the genus Solanum.
Avoiding indiscriminate removal of non-host weeds when they support natural enemies.
